student_response:
Before 1848, the United States had already been moving west under Manifest Destiny. Settlers crossed earlier boundaries, and the government made agreements with Native nations while still wanting more land. The war with Mexico left the United States holding a huge western territory where other communities were already living. This was the background for expansion after 1848.

United States western expansion harmed both Indigenous and Mexican American communities through loss of land and power, but Indigenous communities faced more direct military pressure and reservations while Mexican Americans more often faced legal and economic dispossession.

Many names and events belong to the period. The Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo and its promises. The California Land Act of 1851. Californios, Tejanos, old land grants, court fees, taxes, squatters, and ranches changing hands. The Homestead Act. The transcontinental railroad. Mining camps, cattle trails, federal territories, and new towns. More surveyors and land claims.

There were also the Treaty of Fort Laramie, the Sand Creek Massacre, Red Cloud's War, and the Battle of Little Bighorn. Reservations and federal agencies. The buffalo herds disappearing. The Nez Perce flight and Chief Joseph. Soldiers, forts, ration supplies, boarding schools, and allotment. The Dawes Act came later in the 1880s but is also connected to western land policy. Broken agreements. Smaller territory.

Other items include Chinese railroad workers, military campaigns, territorial courts, vigilantes, irrigation, and railroad land grants. New maps and property lines. Families moving into older communities. Violence in some areas and court cases in others. A lot of western land became controlled by newcomers or federal officials.

By 1877 the notes contain warfare, reservations, laws, debts, taxes, settlers, land offices, and federal officials. Communities across the West had many disputes over land. A final crowded list from the period.

Class notes also mention federal land offices, reservation agents, the Bozeman Trail, the Long Walk, Fort Laramie, California ranches, and New Mexico villages. More policies and conflicts during a crowded period of western settlement.

After the Civil War the United States expanded railroads and national markets. Western land was farmed in larger amounts, cities needed food, and money and credit connected farmers to distant eastern businesses. This was the background for organized farm protest in the late 1800s.

Agrarian protest grew from farmers being squeezed by railroad charges, low crop prices, debt, and a money system they thought favored bankers, so they formed groups that demanded goverment changes. The protests involved both the daily cost of farming and the larger political system.

The Grange, started by Oliver Hudson Kelley. Granger laws and Munn v. Illinois. Farmers Alliances in the South and West. Cooperative stores, lectures, newspapers, and local meetings. The Colored Farmers Alliance. The Populist Party and its Omaha Platform in 1892. Mary Elizabeth Lease. The Ocala Demands. The subtreasury plan.

There were many economic terms in the speeches. Freight rates, grain elevator fees, mortgages, crop liens, tenancy, deflation, and low cotton prices. Greenbacks and free silver. The Sherman Silver Purchase Act. Government ownership of railroads. Direct election of senators. An income tax. Postal savings banks.

The Panic of 1893, Coxey's Army, William Jennings Bryan, and the Cross of Gold speech. Silver at sixteen to one. Populists, Democrats, fusion, and the election of 1896. The gold standard on the other side. Conventions and campaign newspapers. Some farmers joined and some did not.

Railroads, banks, currency, farmers, and politicians appeared in the speeches. The Grange, cooperatives, alliances, and the Populist Party appeared at different times. Many organizations, many demands, and not much agreement about every detail.

The Civil War created a more national economy. After it ended, railroads stretched across the country and farms became tied to distant markets. Homesteaders and former plantation regions produced crops for sale. Banks in eastern cities supplied credit, while factories and cities increased the demand for food and raw materials. Rural families were part of a larger commercial system by the late nineteenth century.

Organized agrarian protest was caused by the financial pressure of commercial farming and by farmers belief that railroads, banks, and national politicians favored other interests. Low crop prices, railroad charges, debt, and tight money were the major causes claimed by farm organizations.

The Grange began in 1867 under Oliver Hudson Kelley. It held meetings and supported cooperative stores. State Granger laws regulated warehouses and railroad rates. Munn v. Illinois upheld an Illinois regulation. Wabash v. Illinois limited state regulation of interstate railroads. The Interstate Commerce Act created a federal commission. These laws and cases appeared repeatedly in farm newspapers and speeches.

The Greenback Labor Party demanded more paper currency. Deflation and old mortgages appeared in farm speeches. The Southern Farmers Alliance, Northwestern Farmers Alliance, and Colored Farmers Alliance organized local chapters. The alliances ran newspapers, arranged lectures, and tried cooperative exchanges. The Colored Farmers Alliance included Black farmers dealing with tenancy and crop liens. Credit and money were repeated subjects at their meetings.

The Ocala Demands, the subtreasury plan, Mary Elizabeth Lease, and Charles Macune. The Populist Party met and issued the Omaha Platform in 1892. It called for free silver, a graduated income tax, direct election of senators, and government ownership of railroads and telegraphs. James B. Weaver ran for president. The Panic of 1893 came next. Coxey's Army marched in 1894.

In 1896 William Jennings Bryan gave the Cross of Gold speech. Populists supported Bryan through fusion with the Democrats. Silver at sixteen to one and the gold standard. Mark Hanna and William McKinley. Bryan lost the election.

The period included clubs, cooperatives, conventions, laws, court cases, third parties, and presidential campaigns. Railroad regulation and currency reform were repeated demands. Debt, low prices, freight costs, banks, and political distrust stayed on the list through the 1890s.

There were also Farmers Mutual Benefit Associations, rural newspapers, traveling lecturers, county conventions, and state tickets. The Southern Alliance spread into former plantation areas. The Northwestern Alliance appeared across plains states. Local cooperatives sometimes failed. Leaders argued over race, fusion, and whether to stay independent from the major parties. More meetings and platforms before the national election.

After the Civil War, national markets grew as railroads linked western resources, factories, and eastern cities. Immigration and urban growth supplied workers and consumers. The federal government encouraged business with tariffs and railroad land grants. Investment banks handled larger amounts of money. This was the setting for industrial growth after 1865.

Industrial consolidation was caused by expensive national production, competition between firms, and weak government regulation, while new transportation and finance allowed a few companies to control large parts of an industry.

Andrew Carnegie used vertical integration in steel. Carnegie Steel owned mines, coke supplies, rail connections, and mills. One large company contained several stages of production. John D. Rockefeller's Standard Oil used horizontal integration. Standard Oil bought refiners, made trust agreements, and received railroad rebates. It held most oil refining at its height. These examples support the growth of large consolidated firms.

J. P. Morgan reorganized railroads and brought companies under bankers supervision. Pools, trusts, holding companies, mergers, stocks, and corporate boards. The Pennsylvania Railroad. Western Union. The American Sugar Refining Company. Gustavus Swift's meatpacking system. National advertising and new distribution networks.

The Sherman Antitrust Act was passed in 1890. United States v. E. C. Knight treated sugar refining as separate from interstate commerce, leaving the combination in place. Laissez faire and Social Darwinisim were common defenses of wealthy industrialists. Courts often protected contracts and property.

The New Deal later created the Securities and Exchange Commission, which does not fit the dates but sometimes gets mixed into business regulation. During the actual period, Carnegie Steel, Standard Oil, Morgan's railroad combinations, trusts, rebates, and E. C. Knight all support the pattern of industrial consolidation.

United States overseas expansion from 1880 to 1898 was when the country became more active outside its own borders. People read about foreign affairs and argued about whether the nation should have influence far away. The government had ships, representatives, and armed forces involved in different situations. There were also businesses trading overseas.

Some people supported expansion and other people opposed it. Newspapers printed exciting stories. Officials gave speeches. Military planners discussed oceans and bases. Religious people discussed missions. Business people discussed goods and customers. These were subjects in public debate, and they appeared often near the end of the century.

The country had more contact with territories and governments beyond its borders. Sometimes it used agreements and sometimes force. There were disputes, fighting, trade, and political arguments. The public did not always know exactly what was happening.

By 1898 the United States was more involved overseas. It had ships going farther, officials paying attention to foreign questions, and debates over territory. Overseas expansion was a major issue during these years and continued to be discussed afterward.

Systems of racial control in the New South and the trans-Mississippi West were similar in using white power, unequal laws, violence, and economic dependence, but the southern system emphasized political and social separation while the western system emphasized control over land and labor.

There were rules about voting, work, schools, travel, housing, land, and public behavior. Some were written and others were customs. Officials could pretend a rule applied equally even when it did not. Employers could decide who got better jobs. Landowners could set debts and payments. Courts usually listened more to white property owners. People who objected could lose work or face threats.

In one town the problem might be voting. In another it might be a school or neighborhood. Somewhere else, land boundaries or low paid work. Different minority communities, different local officials, and different rules. White residents still held most offices and property. Federal officials were around in some western areas, while local officials had more daily control in others.

Violence was part of the system. Threats, mobs, armed groups, arrests, and no punishment afterward. Also contracts that kept workers dependent. Taxes and paperwork. Separate spaces. Families trying to stay on land or keep a business. People moved away, stayed quiet, or resisted in small ways.

The South and West were large regions, so not every place looked exactly alike. The systems were still unequal. Laws, customs, work, land, and force. Most authority remained white, while racial minorities had fewer choices and less protection.

Before 1915, the South had an agricultural economy left damaged by slavery and Reconstruction, while northern cities expanded with industrialization and factory work. Rail lines and national newspapers connected regions more than before. In the West, commercial farming expanded onto dry land with machines and credit.

The Great Migration and Dust Bowl migration were two large movements inside the United States. Many families left homes, looked for work, and entered communities where earlier residents did not always welcome them. There were economic troubles, discrimination, weather problems, jobs, and crowded housing.

Jim Crow, the boll weevil, World War I factory jobs, and the Chicago Defender. Harlem, Chicago, and Detroit. The drought, dust storms, Route 66, and the word Okies. California farm camps and John Steinbeck's The Grapes of Wrath. Trains going north and cars going west. Labor agents, relatives letters, rented rooms, and farm work.

These migrations happened between 1915 and 1940 and moved many people to new parts of the country. New neighborhoods, job competition, music, churches, roadside camps, and schools. Some stayed in the new place and some kept moving. The period had both migrations and many consequences.

In the late 1800s the United States changed from a mostly rural country into an industrial and urban one. Railroads connected a national market, factories employed immigrants, and corporations gained enormous size during the Gilded Age. City goverments had to deal with crowded housing and sanitation. Reconstruction had ended and political parties were often controlled by machines and patronage. This larger change came before Progressive reform.

Progressive reform took place from 1890 to 1920 and involved many problems and many reformers. There was corruption, poverty, bad working conditions, monopolies, alcohol, and the position of women. Reform happened at city, state, and federal levels. Some reformers were middle class and others were workers or activists. It was a period where government did more than before.

There were muckrakers such as Ida Tarbell and Lincoln Steffens. Upton Sinclair wrote The Jungle. The Triangle Shirtwaist Fire happened in New York. Jane Addams operated Hull House in Chicago. Jacob Riis published How the Other Half Lives. The National American Woman Suffrage Association and the Women's Christian Temperance Union. Booker T. Washington and W. E. B. Du Bois disagreed. The NAACP was founded.

Political reforms included the initiative, referendum, recall, and direct primary. The Seventeenth Amendment established direct election of senators. The Nineteenth Amendment concerned woman suffrage. The Eighteenth Amendment established prohibition. Theodore Roosevelt had the Square Deal. The Pure Food and Drug Act and Meat Inspection Act were passed. The Sherman Antitrust Act and Clayton Antitrust Act were also part of regulation. Woodrow Wilson had the New Freedom.

Workers held strikes and unions asked for safer jobs and shorter hours. Cities used commissions and city managers. Conservation became an issue with national forests and parks. There were also settlement houses and public health campaigns. Southern states still had segregation, and many reformers ignored racial injustice or supported it.

Overall, the Progressive Era had industrial conditions, political corruption, social campaigns, and federal laws. Reformers appeared in journalism, workplaces, neighborhoods, and elections. The period included all these developments and ended around the First World War and the constitutional amendmants of the early twentieth century.

The Gilded Age created giant corporations, crowded cities, and political machines while immigration and industrial work grew rapidly. Farmers and workers had already formed protests against railroads, low wages, and concentrated wealth. These developments created the setting for reform after 1890.

Progressive reform was caused by the social damage of industrialization, anger at corrupt politics, and a middle-class belief that experts and stronger government could improve society. Reformers went after business power, city problems, and political rules, although their campaigns were not all the same.

A large number of people and measures were part of the movement. Jacob Riis and How the Other Half Lives. Jane Addams and Hull House. Ida Tarbell's writing on Standard Oil. Upton Sinclair and The Jungle. The Triangle Shirtwaist Fire. Muckrakers, settlement houses, social gospel ministers, and women's clubs. These examples are usually connected to poverty, working conditions, and city reform.

There were political reforms too. Initiative, referendum, recall, and the direct primary. The Seventeenth Amendment for direct election of senators. City manager systems and secret ballots. Robert La Follette in Wisconsin. Theodore Roosevelt's Square Deal and New Nationalism. Woodrow Wilson's New Freedom. The Pure Food and Drug Act, Meat Inspection Act, Federal Reserve Act, and Clayton Antitrust Act. Conservation and the creation of more protected lands.

Women were active in reform organizations. The Women's Christian Temperance Union, National American Woman Suffrage Association, and the Nineteenth Amendment. Temperance also led to the Eighteenth Amendment. Some reformers worked on child labor and hours for women. Muller v. Oregon. Others campaigned against prostitution and for public health.

Race was another issue but was often left out by white Progressives. The NAACP, W. E. B. Du Bois, and the Niagara Movement. Booker T. Washington and the Atlanta Compromise. The Brownsville affair. Segregation still existed and lynching continued despite reform language.

Progressivism therefore came from industrial, urban, and political problems and from reformers confidence in organized action. The era included journalists, social workers, presidents, amendments, and regulatory laws. All of these were parts of Progressive reform from 1890 to 1920.

Long before American entry into World War I, the federal government had restricted liberty during emergencies. The Alien and Sedition Acts punished critics during conflict with France, and Lincoln suspended habeas corpus during the Civil War. Americans also had a tradition of free speech and due process. These competing traditions formed the background to wartime policy after 1917.

From 1917 to 1945, federal restrictions showed continuity because national security repeatedly overrode individual liberty, but they changed from punishment of political speech in World War I to controls over whole populations and information during World War II.

The Espionage Act of 1917 and Sedition Act of 1918. Eugene V. Debs was imprisoned. Schenck v. United States and the phrase clear and present danger. The Committee on Public Information. Limits on antiwar publications, draft resistance, conscientious objectors, and mail inspection. Military zones and loyalty oaths.

After the first war came the first Red Scare, Palmer Raids, arrests, and deportations. In the 1930s, the House Un-American Activities Committee investigated political beliefs. The Smith Act was passed in 1940. Federal departments, judges, local police, and investigators. Hearings and lists of names.

World War II included Executive Order 9066, Japanese American relocation, and camps such as Manzanar. Korematsu v. United States. Curfews and exclusion zones on the West Coast. The Office of Censorship. German and Italian enemy aliens. Military commanders, registration forms, and restricted information.

The period had laws, court cases, prison sentences, investigations, censorship, curfews, and internment. Speeches, radicals, the draft, ancestry, military zones, and relocation camps. Many federal offices and many different restrictions appeared between 1917 and 1945.

More items appeared in each emergency. During the first war, federal prosecutors, draft boards, labor radicals, socialist newspapers, and prison sentences. During the second, registration, travel restrictions, military orders, camp guards, and government-approved news. The Supreme Court heard several cases. Congress passed laws and executive agencies carried them out. Some restrictions ended when war ended, while other investigations continued. Newspapers debated liberty but also printed official warnings. Citizens signed loyalty statements or reported suspicious neighbors. More hearings, forms, and wartime notices.

Earlier American wars produced conflicts between security and liberty. The Alien and Sedition Acts punished criticism during tensions with France, and Lincoln suspended habeas corpus during the Civil War. By 1917, the federal government had precedents for expanding authority when leaders claimed an emergency.

Federal restrictions from 1917 to 1945 were continuous in putting national security above civil liberties, but changed in their main targets: World War I policy focused on antiwar and radical speech, while World War II policy also included mass removal based on ancestry.

Congress passed the Espionage Act of 1917 and Sedition Act of 1918. The laws covered interference with the draft and criticism of the war. Eugene V. Debs received a prison sentence after an antiwar speech. Schenck v. United States upheld a conviction and used the clear and present danger phrase. Political dissent appeared in each of these cases.

Then the Palmer Raids, the first Red Scare, deportations, and loyalty investigations. The Smith Act of 1940. Hearings, government files, and accused radicals.

Executive Order 9066 authorized military exclusion zones during World War II. Japanese American families on the West Coast were sent to camps such as Manzanar. Korematsu v. United States upheld exclusion. Ancestry, location, exclusion, and detention appeared in these policies. The Office of Censorship also reviewed communications.

Laws, courts, prison terms, censorship, curfews, exclusion, and camps. Federal offices and military offices. Security language appeared across the list of policies.

Other items were draft resistance, mail inspection, military zones, loyalty oaths, enemy-alien registration, and restricted newspapers. Different federal offices kept records and enforced the rules. More hearings and federal orders.

The Great Depression left unemployment and unused factory capacity across the United States. New Deal agencies had already increased federal involvement in the economy, and the CCC put young men to work on public projects. War began in Europe before American entry, bringing defense orders and a peacetime draft. This was the setting for mobilization after 1941.

Wartime mobilization caused economic and social change by converting factories to military production, bringing new workers into paid jobs, and increasing federal control over labor and resources. It also changed migration and opportunities for women and minority workers.

The War Production Board directed factories toward weapons, planes, and ships. Automobile plants made military equipment. Industrial output rose and unemployment fell. Factories, jobs, government orders. A large wartime production list. The Office of Price Administration, ration books, price controls, war bonds, and federal contracts. Shipyards and aircraft plants on the West Coast.

Rosie the Riveter represented women in defense jobs. More married women worked for wages. Federal day care centers operated in some areas. Welding, riveting, offices, uniforms. Women leaving jobs when soldiers returned. Short section. A rushed list.

A. Philip Randolph threatened a march on Washington. Executive Order 8802 banned discrimination in defense industries, and the Fair Employment Practices Committee was created. Defense employment and Black workers appeared in the order and committee. The Second Great Migration brought Black families to northern and western defense cities. The Detroit riot also occurred.

The Bracero Program brought Mexican laborers for farm work. Executive Order 9066 removed Japanese Americans and sent families to camps. The GI Bill passed in 1944. Servicemen, factory workers, migrants, rationing, and federal agencies.

The War Production Board, Rosie the Riveter, Executive Order 8802, the Great Migration, internment, and the Bracero Program. Economic production, migration, jobs, federal rules, and unequal treatment during the war.

Other wartime items filled the homefront: victory gardens, scrap drives, war bonds, ration coupons, wage controls, labor disputes, and crowded boomtown housing. Factories ran extra shifts. Families moved for defense jobs. Soldiers left civilian work. Government posters appeared everywhere.

Containment policy from 1945 to 1960 was a foreign policy about keeping another system from spreading. The United States had disagreements with another powerful country. Officials made speeches, sent money, met with allies, and increased military planning. There were tense situations and fighting in different regions.

People at home worried about danger and loyalty. Employees were investigated and citizens accused each other of holding dangerous ideas. Leaders talked about freedom and friendly governments. There were economic actions, military actions, and secret actions. The policy was in newspapers and political speeches.

Later wars from another decade sometimes get mixed into this subject even though they happened after 1960. There were soldiers in distant areas, arguments at home, and government officials making decisions.

Containment continued through these years. It involved aid, agreements, weapons, threats, and many meetings. The United States became involved in more foreign problems. It was an important policy and people disagreed about it, but the details can be confusing.

The modern environmental movement from 1945 to 1980 grew because pollution became more visible, suburban and industrial growth damaged nature, and ordinary people became worried about health and limited resources. Greater expectations for government action also mattered.

More cars. More roads and houses. Factories making chemicals and consumer products. Smoke in the air and waste in water. Dead animals, dirty neighborhoods, strange smells, and sickness. Books and television reports about nature. Students and families at public events. Not everyone agreeing.

There were conservation groups, neighborhood meetings, recycling drives, and demands for cleaner air and water. Arguments about pesticides, energy, wilderness, garbage, and population. Officials creating offices and rules. Businesses complaining about costs. Citizens writing letters and holding signs.

Postwar growth and pollution were talked about across the country. Health fears and concern for nature. More attention in schools and news reports. The movement became larger by 1980, with many types of people involved. Several concerns at once. Local problems and national attention.

After World War II, the United States and Soviet Union became rival superpowers. Containment shaped foreign affairs, while fear of communism affected politics at home. Loyalty investigations, atomic anxiety, and arguments over presidential power formed the broader Cold War setting.

The Korean and Vietnam Wars were both connected to domestic politics between 1950 and 1975. Military leadership, communism, elections, protest, and presidential authority were all discussed. Public support and opposition also appeared.

Truman's decision to fight under the United Nations. General Douglas MacArthur and Truman's firing of him. No formal declaration of war. The 1952 election and Eisenhower's promise to go to Korea. McCarthyism in the early 1950s. An armistice instead of a peace treaty.

The Gulf of Tonkin Resolution, Lyndon Johnson's Great Society, the Tet Offensive, and the Pentagon Papers. The Democratic convention in Chicago in 1968. Students for a Democratic Society and the Kent State shootings. Richard Nixon's silent majority. The War Powers Act. The Twenty-sixth Amendment.

Television coverage, casualties, the draft, taxes, and demonstrations. Election posters and antiwar signs. Officials making speeches and citizens distrusting them. Congressional votes, campus meetings, and newspaper reports. Political events from the 1950s through the 1970s.

Elections, presidential war powers, MacArthur, the Gulf of Tonkin Resolution, Tet, Kent State, and the War Powers Act. All were domestic political subjects during these years.

Other political terms included limited war, containment, credibility, the credibility gap, hawks and doves, peace candidates, congressional hearings, and campaign promises. Different words in different years. More speeches, petitions, opinion polls, newspaper columns, and campaign stops.

After World War II the United States and Soviet Union became competing superpowers, with nuclear weapons, rival alliances, and struggles in other countries. The Berlin blockade, NATO, and the Cuban missile crisis were earlier parts of this long conflict, and by the 1970s leaders had also tried detente.

The end of the Cold War happened from 1980 to 1991 and a lot changed in a short time, there were speeches, military spending, reform ideas, unstable governments, meetings, protests, elections, and news reports from many countries.

Ronald Reagan called the Soviet Union an evil empire. The Strategic Defense Initiative. Mikhail Gorbachev announced glasnost and perestroika. The Solidarity movement in Poland. The fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989. Elections in eastern Europe. The failed coup in Moscow and the breakup of the Soviet Union in 1991.

There were economic problems and protests, also arms talks and new leaders, and citizens saw changes in nearby countries. These were events in the final Cold War years.

The Cold War began after World War II when the United States and Soviet Union competed over Europe, nuclear weapons, and different political systems. Containment, NATO, the Warsaw Pact, and crises in Berlin and Cuba made the rivalry last for decades, although detente in the 1970s lowered some tensions.

From 1980 to 1991 the Cold War ended because Soviet economic weakness, pressure from the United States, and reform movements reduced Soviet control and made the old system fall apart. These factors all mattered to the sudden end.

There was Ronald Reagan's military buildup and Strategic Defense Initiative, he also gave the evil empire speech and spoke at the Berlin Wall, defense spending increased and relations were tense early in the decade. Mikhail Gorbachev came to power. Glasnost and perestroika. The INF Treaty. The Soviet war in Afghanistan.

In eastern Europe there was the Solidarity movement in Poland, protests, elections, and governments losing control. The Berlin Wall fell in 1989. Germany reunified. There were nationalist movements inside the Soviet republics. A coup failed in 1991, and the Soviet Union dissolved.

The economy, arms race, Reagan, Gorbachev, protests, and nationalism all appear in the period, there were speeches and treaties beside elections and street protests. By 1991 the Soviet state had broken apart.

The civil rights movement of the 1950s and 1960s gave later movements examples of marches, court cases, direct action, and federal legislation. By the 1980s, rights campaigns also worked through national media and nonprofit organizations. Conservative politics created resistance involving sexuality and immigration.

LGBTQ rights and immigrant-rights movements both used demonstrations, legal challenges, and community organizations, but LGBTQ activists focused more on recognition and equal treatment while immigrant activists focused more on legal status, deportation, and workplace rights.

The Immigration Reform and Control Act of 1986, local immigrant organizations, and labor unions. ACT UP held confrontational demonstrations during the AIDS crisis and demanded medical attention. Pride marches continued in major cities. Public protest was visible in these events.

Legal organizations challenged sodomy laws. Lawrence v. Texas struck those laws down in 2003. Marriage campaigns moved through state courts and elections. The 2006 immigration marches brought millions into streets against a restrictive House bill. Mass public action was visible in those marches.

DREAMers held sit-ins and public campaigns for undocumented young people. DACA later gave some temporary protection. Churches, worker centers, and local groups opposed deportations. LGBTQ legal campaigns reached Obergefell v. Hodges in 2015. Court briefs, rallies, lobbying, media interviews, and coalition groups.

ACT UP, Lawrence, immigrant marches, DREAMers, DACA, and Obergefell. Court briefs, public events, sit-ins, parades, lobbying, and media campaigns. A long list of legal action and direct action from 1980 to 2015.

Earlier in the twentieth century, the New Deal and World War II created broad support for a larger federal government. That agreement weakened during the civil rights era, Vietnam, and the cultural conflicts of the 1960s and 1970s. Regional party loyalties shifted, and conservatives built a national movement before 1980.

Growing polarization from 1980 to 2020 was caused by ideological sorting between the parties, conflicts over race and culture, and a changing media system that rewarded partisan conflict. Campaign organizations and district boundaries also favored loyal party voters.

Ronald Reagan won in 1980 with conservative positions on taxes, religion, and anticommunisim. Southern white voters increasingly voted Republican while many minority voters remained Democratic. Fewer liberal Republicans and conservative Democrats. Abortion, guns, affirmative action, and gay rights became party issues. Party labels and voting groups changed during these years.

Newt Gingrich and the 1992 Contract with America. Government shutdowns under Bill Clinton. Clinton's impeachment. The 2000 election and Bush v. Gore. Party votes and harsh campaign advertisements. Safe districts and primary elections.

Cable news developed partisan audiences. Fox News on the right and later MSNBC on the left. Internet sites, political blogs, and social media. Barack Obama's election, the Tea Party, and fights over health care. Different audiences followed different political coverage.

Donald Trump's election put immigration and national identity at the center of politics. Black Lives Matter and arguments over policing. The Citizens United decision in 2008, outside spending, ideological donors, and gerrymandering. Rural and urban voting differences. Religious and educational differences.

By 2020 the list included party sorting, culture issues, cable news, social media, donors, district lines, the Tea Party, and Trump. Elections, campaigns, congressional votes, and ordinary governing.

More party-line voting and fewer politicians crossing over. Constant fundraising, televised debates, online arguments, and nationalized local elections.

The economic policies of the two administrations from 1981 to 2008 involved taxes, spending, business, and the national economy, there were arguments about whether government should do more or less and people had different opinions when the economy was good or bad for them.

One administration came earlier and the other came later. Each administration had plans for money and the economy. Supporters said the plans helped business and let people keep money, critics said wealthy people got more benefits and government debt got worse, the economy grew sometimes and had problems sometimes.

There were changes in jobs, trade, technology, housing, and prices. Presidents talked about growth and freedom. Lawmakers debated budgets. Businesses wanted fewer rules while workers worried about wages and security. Wars cost money and affected spending.

Near the end there were serious financial problems and arguments about what government should do. Banks and homeowners were in trouble. Officials held meetings and proposed large actions. The economic policies from these years are still debated, and people disagree over what worked.
